subtopic_detail: Kawasaki disease treatment within ten days of fever onset
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 3-year-old boy on day 6 of fever above 39 °C. He has a polymorphous truncal rash, bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, a strawberry tongue and a unilateral cervical lymph node measuring 1.7 cm. His hands and feet are erythematous and swollen. He has had no diarrhoea and his immunisations are current. Throat swab for group A streptococcus is negative.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 °C, HR 156, BP 95/58, RR 26, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Polymorphous rash, bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis, cracked lips, strawberry tongue, 1.7 cm right cervical node, swollen hands and feet, no peeling yet
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L, WCC 16.4 x10^9/L, platelets 540 x10^9/L, CRP 142 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/h, ALT 92 U/L, albumin 28 g/L, urinalysis: sterile pyuria. Throat swab negative. Echocardiogram requested.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Start oral amoxicillin 50 mg/kg daily for ten days and review for clinical response and rash resolution at the end of the antibiotic course
options.rationale: The clinical picture and a negative throat swab do not support streptococcal infection; antibiotics will not address the underlying vasculitis or prevent coronary artery aneurysms. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Reassure the family that this is likely a viral exanthem with reactive cervical lymphadenitis and discharge home with simple antipyretic advice and review
options.rationale: Five days of fever plus four of the five clinical criteria meet the threshold for Kawasaki disease and inflammatory markers support active vasculitis; discharge risks untreated coronary artery aneurysms. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Order an echocardiogram first and defer all systemic treatment until coronary artery dimensions are formally confirmed by the paediatric cardiology service
options.rationale: Treatment is not contingent on echocardiographic findings; delaying intravenous immunoglobulin past day 10 increases the risk of coronary artery aneurysms. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Admit for intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as a single infusion plus oral aspirin 3 to 5 mg/kg daily, with echocardiography arranged within the admission
options.rationale: He meets clinical criteria for complete Kawasaki disease; intravenous immunoglobulin within the first 10 days of fever plus low-dose aspirin reduces coronary artery aneurysm rates from approximately 25% to under 5%. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence high-dose oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg daily monotherapy and arrange outpatient paediatric rheumatology follow-up within five working days of presentation
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are an adjunct considered for IVIG resistance or high-risk patients, not as monotherapy; standard initial treatment is intravenous immunoglobulin with aspirin. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a medium-vessel vasculitis of childhood and the leading cause of acquired heart disease in Australian children. The diagnosis requires at least 5 days of fever plus 4 of 5 clinical features: bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis, oral changes, polymorphous rash, peripheral changes, and cervical lymphadenopathy. Treatment with intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg plus aspirin within the first 10 days of fever reduces coronary artery aneurysm rates from around 25% to under 5%. Sterile pyuria, raised transaminases and low albumin support the diagnosis even when classical features are partial.
key_points: Five days of fever plus 4 of 5 clinical criteria establishes complete Kawasaki disease
Intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg plus aspirin reduces aneurysm rates from around 25% to under 5%
Treatment within the first 10 days of fever is the key prognostic determinant for coronary outcomes
Sterile pyuria, raised liver enzymes and low albumin are supportive features
Refractory disease at 36 hours after IVIG triggers a second dose plus corticosteroid escalation
explanation.pearls: Adjunctive corticosteroids are considered up front for children with predicted IVIG resistance based on the Kobayashi or Sano scores in Japanese cohorts, although routine use in Australian children remains case-by-case.

subtopic_detail: Neonatal jaundice with ABO incompatibility and phototherapy threshold
stem: You are the neonatal resident reviewing a 38-hour-old term boy who appears jaundiced down to his upper thighs. He was born at 39 weeks by normal vaginal delivery to a healthy mother whose blood group is O Rh-positive. The baby's blood group is A Rh-positive. He is breastfeeding 3 to 4 hourly with good attachment. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 138, RR 46, SpO2 98% room air, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Alert, vigorous suck, well perfused, jaundiced to upper thighs, no scalp haematoma, no hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Total serum bilirubin 305 micromol/L (conjugated 12 micromol/L), Hb 158 g/L, reticulocytes 5%, direct antiglobulin test weakly positive, blood group A Rh-positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue routine breastfeeding without phototherapy or specific intervention and arrange a community midwife review at 72 hours of life for jaundice
options.rationale: His bilirubin sits well above the Australian phototherapy threshold for a well term baby at 38 hours, and ABO incompatibility raises the risk of rapid bilirubin acceleration; observation alone is unsafe. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guidelines: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Switch the infant to formula feeding for 24 hours, withhold breastfeeding and reassess the bilirubin trend after one full day of replacement feeds only
options.rationale: Routine cessation of breastfeeding is not indicated for haemolytic jaundice; supplementation may be considered for hydration but the underlying ABO incompatibility still requires phototherapy. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guidelines: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Commence intensive phototherapy, continue breastfeeding with supplementation if intake is inadequate and recheck bilirubin and haematocrit in 4 to 6 hours
options.rationale: His bilirubin exceeds the phototherapy line on the term low-risk nomogram at 38 hours, and the positive direct antiglobulin test with ABO mismatch is a risk factor for rapid rise; intensive phototherapy with hydration is standard. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guidelines: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Arrange immediate double-volume exchange transfusion in the neonatal intensive care unit as the initial intervention for haemolytic neonatal hyperbilirubinaemia
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is reserved for bilirubin levels above the exchange line, signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy or rapid rise on intensive phototherapy; the first step is intensive phototherapy. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guidelines: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Administer a single dose of intravenous immunoglobulin 0.5 g/kg now and recheck the total bilirubin in six hours before deciding on additional phototherapy
options.rationale: Intravenous immunoglobulin is an adjunct considered only when bilirubin continues to rise despite intensive phototherapy and approaches the exchange transfusion line, not as an initial intervention. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guidelines: Neonatal Jaundice)

explanation.summary: Neonatal jaundice plotted against postnatal hours on the Australian phototherapy nomogram is the basis of management decisions. ABO incompatibility with a positive direct antiglobulin test is an acceleration risk and lowers the threshold for intensive phototherapy. Exchange transfusion is reserved for levels above the exchange line, signs of bilirubin-induced neurological dysfunction or failure of intensive phototherapy. Intravenous immunoglobulin reduces the need for exchange transfusion in isoimmune disease when bilirubin is climbing toward the exchange line.
key_points: Bilirubin must always be plotted against postnatal hours on a treatment nomogram
ABO incompatibility with positive direct antiglobulin test is a risk factor for rapid haemolysis
Intensive phototherapy is first-line and exchange transfusion is reserved for failure or extreme levels
Intravenous immunoglobulin is adjunctive in isoimmune haemolytic disease at high bilirubin levels
Hydration with breast or expressed milk is continued throughout phototherapy whenever possible
explanation.pearls: A rise of more than 8.5 micromol/L per hour or failure to respond to intensive phototherapy is the trigger to consider intravenous immunoglobulin and prepare for exchange transfusion.

subtopic_detail: Group B streptococcus intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis
stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 32-year-old primigravida at 39+2 weeks in early spontaneous labour. Her membranes ruptured 4 hours ago and the liquor is clear. Her routine 36-week vaginal-rectal swab was positive for group B streptococcus. She has no penicillin allergy. Her observations are stable and the cardiotocograph is reassuring.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, fully effaced, vertex at -1 station, contractions every 4 minutes
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal-rectal swab at 36 weeks: GBS positive. FBC and group and screen unremarkable.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum management?

options.text: Defer antibiotic therapy and reassess the need for prophylaxis only if maternal temperature rises above 38 °C or fetal tachycardia develops during labour progress
options.rationale: Universal intrapartum prophylaxis for GBS carriers is recommended in Australia regardless of intrapartum temperature; antibiotics are not contingent on developing chorioamnionitis. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose followed by 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery and document the time of the first dose for paediatrics
options.rationale: Intrapartum benzylpenicillin 3 g loading and 1.8 g 4-hourly is the Australian first-line regimen for a GBS-colonised labouring woman without penicillin allergy; documenting the dose time is essential for the newborn risk pathway. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Start intravenous vancomycin 1 g 12-hourly empirically because of the rising prevalence of macrolide and lincosamide resistance in Australian GBS isolates currently
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for women with high-risk penicillin allergy or proven clindamycin resistance; it is not first-line for a woman with no penicillin allergy. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Offer a single dose of oral cefalexin 2 g now and arrange ongoing 8-hourly dosing throughout labour with neonatal observation in the maternity ward post-delivery
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not achieve reliable intra-amniotic concentrations for prophylaxis; intravenous administration is required. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Recommend immediate caesarean section before active labour establishes to avoid the need for any intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis for this colonised pregnancy
options.rationale: GBS colonisation alone is not an indication for caesarean section; prelabour caesarean section with intact membranes is the only delivery mode that does not require intrapartum prophylaxis. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus)

explanation.summary: Maternal GBS colonisation is the leading cause of early-onset neonatal sepsis in Australia. Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis with intravenous benzylpenicillin reduces vertical transmission and early-onset disease by around 80%. The Australian universal screening approach uses a vaginal-rectal swab at 35 to 37 weeks; risk-based approaches without screening are still used in some centres. The time of the first antibiotic dose feeds directly into the neonatal observation pathway after delivery.
key_points: Benzylpenicillin 3 g intravenous loading then 1.8 g 4-hourly is first line for women without penicillin allergy
Cefazolin is used for low-risk penicillin allergy and clindamycin or vancomycin for high-risk allergy
Adequate prophylaxis means at least one dose 4 hours or more before delivery
Prelabour caesarean section with intact membranes does not require intrapartum prophylaxis
Intrapartum fever above 38 °C triggers broader spectrum antibiotics for chorioamnionitis cover
explanation.pearls: A neonatal early-onset sepsis risk calculator is used at term to integrate maternal antibiotic timing, GBS status and clinical findings into observation versus active treatment decisions.

subtopic_detail: First-line medical management of heavy menstrual bleeding
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 28-year-old nulliparous woman with two years of heavy menstrual bleeding. She floods through a pad every 90 minutes on her heaviest day and feels light-headed by day 3. Her cycles are regular at 28 days. She has no intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding. She would prefer to retain her fertility for the future. She has no contraindications to combined hormonal contraception.

data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, abdomen soft, bimanual examination unremarkable, no adnexal mass
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, MCV 72 fL, ferritin 7 micrograms/L, TSH 1.4 mIU/L, beta-HCG negative. Pelvic ultrasound: normal-sized uterus, no fibroids or polyps, normal endometrium 6 mm.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line medical management?

options.text: Schedule endometrial ablation at the next available list and start oral iron replacement therapy in the interim while she awaits the procedure date
options.rationale: Endometrial ablation is reserved for women who have completed childbearing and have failed medical therapy; it is not appropriate when fertility preservation is desired. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Refer for outpatient hysteroscopy and dilatation and curettage as the first-line investigation and treatment before any medical therapy is initiated for this complaint
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy is indicated for abnormal ultrasound findings, persistent intermenstrual bleeding or failed medical therapy, not as first-line management of regular heavy menstrual bleeding with a normal ultrasound. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Commence cyclical norethisterone 5 mg three times daily from day 5 to day 26 of each menstrual cycle and review the response in three months from initiation
options.rationale: Cyclical oral progestogens are less effective than the levonorgestrel intrauterine device and are reserved for women who decline an intrauterine device or have contraindications. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Recommend definitive laparoscopic hysterectomy as the most effective and durable treatment option for refractory heavy menstrual bleeding in a woman of her age group
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is a definitive option but is reserved for completed families and failed medical therapy; it does not preserve fertility. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device after counselling on initial irregular bleeding and start oral iron 100 mg elemental daily for the deficiency
options.rationale: The 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine device reduces menstrual blood loss by around 90% and is first-line medical therapy for heavy menstrual bleeding in Australia; it preserves fertility and treats coexisting iron deficiency anaemia. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

explanation.summary: Heavy menstrual bleeding affects around one in four women in Australia and is a major cause of iron deficiency anaemia. The 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine device is first-line medical therapy because it reduces blood loss by about 90% and preserves fertility. Tranexamic acid and non-steroidal anti-inflammatories are useful when an intrauterine device is declined or unsuitable. Surgical options are reserved for completed families or failed medical therapy.
key_points: Levonorgestrel intrauterine device reduces menstrual blood loss by around 90%
Combined oral contraceptive pill, tranexamic acid and non-steroidal anti-inflammatories are second-line non-invasive options
Endometrial ablation and hysterectomy are reserved for completed families or failed medical therapy
Iron deficiency anaemia from blood loss requires concurrent iron replacement
Ultrasound is performed to exclude fibroids, polyps and endometrial thickening before treatment
explanation.pearls: Counsel women that irregular bleeding is common in the first three to six months after a levonorgestrel intrauterine device insertion, peaking at four months before settling into oligomenorrhoea or amenorrhoea.

subtopic_detail: Routine folic acid supplementation for low-risk preconception care
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 29-year-old healthy woman attending for preconception advice. She has a regular cycle, no chronic conditions and is on no regular medications. She has no personal or family history of neural tube defects. Her body mass index is 23 kg/m^2 and she does not smoke or drink alcohol. She hopes to conceive within the next six months.

data_table.Investigations: Up-to-date cervical screening test negative for HPV, rubella immune, varicella immune, syphilis non-reactive, HIV negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate preconception supplementation advice?

options.text: Start folic acid 400 micrograms daily and iodine 150 micrograms daily now and continue through the first trimester of pregnancy at the same daily doses
options.rationale: Folic acid 400 micrograms daily for at least one month before conception through the first trimester reduces neural tube defects, and iodine 150 micrograms daily is recommended throughout preconception, pregnancy and lactation in Australia. (Source: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy Counselling)

options.text: Defer all vitamin supplementation until a positive pregnancy test and commence routine folic acid then together with a standard prenatal multivitamin preparation
options.rationale: Folic acid given only after a positive pregnancy test misses the critical neural tube closure window at 21 to 28 days post-conception; supplementation must precede conception. (Source: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy Counselling)

options.text: Recommend high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily for at least three months before conception and continue this throughout the first trimester for routine neural tube defect prevention
options.rationale: High-dose folic acid 5 mg is reserved for higher-risk women: previous neural tube defect, diabetes, antiepileptic use, body mass index over 30, or family history; not for low-risk women. (Source: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy Counselling)

options.text: Start vitamin A 10,000 international units daily, combined vitamin E and selenium and a high-dose marine omega-3 supplement during preconception planning each day
options.rationale: High-dose vitamin A is teratogenic in early pregnancy and routine omega-3 or antioxidant supplementation has no fertility or anomaly-prevention benefit at this dose. (Source: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy Counselling)

options.text: Commence elemental iron 100 mg daily plus calcium 1000 mg daily as universal preconception measures, with folic acid only added after she becomes pregnant
options.rationale: Routine elemental iron and calcium are not universally recommended preconception in women without deficiency; folic acid must precede conception to be effective. (Source: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy Counselling)

explanation.summary: Routine preconception supplementation in Australia comprises folic acid 400 micrograms daily and iodine 150 micrograms daily. Folic acid must start at least one month before conception and continue through the first trimester to span the neural tube closure window. Iodine continues throughout pregnancy and lactation because of the increased thyroid demand and reduced placental iodine reserves. High-dose folic acid 5 mg is reserved for higher-risk women, including those with previous neural tube defects, diabetes, antiepileptic use or body mass index over 30.
key_points: Folic acid 400 micrograms daily preconception through first trimester is the routine dose
Iodine 150 micrograms daily is recommended preconception through pregnancy and lactation
High-dose folic acid 5 mg is reserved for previous neural tube defect, diabetes, antiepileptics or BMI over 30
Vitamin A above 10,000 international units daily is teratogenic and should be avoided
Rubella, varicella and pertussis serology should be checked and updated preconception
explanation.pearls: Women on valproate or carbamazepine require 5 mg folic acid preconception and ongoing antiepileptic review by a neurologist because of the high risk of neural tube and other major malformations.
